title: Josephson spectroscopy study of kagome superconductors toward the deep point-contact regime
authors: Hailang Qin, Xiao-Yu Yan, Hanbin Deng, Mu-Wei Gao, Guowei Liu, Yuanyuan Zhao, Jia-Xin Yin
abstract: Josephson scanning tunneling microscopy (JSTM) has emerged as an important technique for probing the superconducting order parameter at the atomic scale. However, the Josephson current in JSTM may behave quite differently when the coupling strength varies. Here, we push the junction to the deep point-contact regime, reaching a normal-state junction resistance of only 0.15 $h/2e^2 \simeq 2~{\rm k}Ω$. We demonstrate, using kagome superconductors, that the zero-bias conductance, a key characteristic of the Josephson current, deviates strongly from the quadratic dependence on the normal-state conductance upon entering the deep point-contact regime. Furthermore, we observe a striking saturation of the zero-bias conductance, which we show arises from the series resistance in the circuit. This also serves as a cautious reminder when interpreting zero-bias conductance saturation or quantization in studies of exotic physics such as that of Majorana zero modes if the tip-sample junction resistance is extremely small. Finally, we identify an optimum regime where JSTM can be used as an atomic-scale probe for studying pair-density wave states in materials with low superconducting transition temperature, such as AV3Sb5 kagome superconductors.
summary: The paper explores the transition of the Josephson effect from tunneling to the deep point-contact regime in kagome superconductors. It reveals that observed conductance saturation at high coupling is an artifact of series resistance, providing a cautionary note for interpreting exotic physics. Finally, it identifies the best regime for using JSTM to study pair-density wave states.
main_problem: Investigating the behavior of the Josephson current and zero-bias conductance (ZBC) in JSTM as the junction transitions from the tunneling regime to the deep point-contact regime, and identifying measurement artifacts.
main_result: The study demonstrates that ZBC saturation at extremely low junction resistances is an experimental artifact caused by series resistance, and identifies an optimal conductance regime for probing pair-density wave states.
method: Josephson scanning tunneling microscopy (JSTM) using superconducting tips on kagome superconductors, involving $dI/dV$ spectroscopy, $I-z$ spectroscopy, and calibration of series resistance.
model_or_system: Kagome superconductors, specifically Cs(V0.86Ta0.14)3Sb5 and KV3Sb5, studied using a superconducting Nb or Pt-Ir tip in a dilution refrigerator.
key_observables: Zero-bias conductance (ZBC), normal-state conductance (Gn), superconducting gap (delta), and critical current (Ic).
important_parameters: Tunneling regime (Gn <= G0), crossover/point-contact regime (G0 <= Gn <= 2G0), and deep point-contact regime (Gn > 2G0); series resistance (Rseries).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the ZBC saturation is driven by external circuit resistance rather than intrinsic physics like Majorana zero modes.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Comparison of spectra with superconducting vs non-superconducting tips; Fig 2: I-V and dI/dV spectra across varying resistances; Fig 3: ZBC vs Gn relationship and topographic surface modification; Fig 4: Investigation of optimal regime for PDW probing.
paper_structure: Introduction to JSTM; experimental setup and calibration; exploration of conductance regimes via I-V and I-z spectroscopy; identification of ZBC saturation artifact; demonstration of optimal regime for PDW mapping.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
